s: Old prison, new court — where Mandela was held
w: Built as a fort in 1896, then a prison where Gandhi, Mandela and Oliver Tambo all did time. The Old Fort's Number Four block held black prisoners; the Women's Jail held suffragists and anti-apartheid activists. In 2004 the Constitutional Court — built with bricks from the demolished cells — opened on the same ground.
_facts: Built 1896 as a fort, converted to prison shortly after
Gandhi, Mandela, and Tambo were all imprisoned here at different times
Number Four block specifically held Black prisoners
Constitutional Court opened 2004 on the same site, using bricks from demolished cells

w: On 16 June 1976, police opened fire on schoolchildren protesting compulsory Afrikaans instruction. Hector Pieterson, age 12, was among the first killed. Sam Nzima's photograph — his body carried by a classmate, sister running beside — circled the globe and broke something open. Two blocks from where it happened.
_facts: Museum located in Orlando West, Soweto, two blocks from where Pieterson was shot
Hector Pieterson was 12 years old when shot on 16 June 1976
Sam Nzima's photograph became the iconic image of the uprising
More than 170 protesting schoolchildren were killed in the Soweto uprising

s: Mine shaft entrance to the worst of it
w: Visitors are assigned a race on entry — "White" or "Non-White" — and directed through separate gates into the same exhibition. Beside the old Crown Mines shaft, it runs through segregation, resistance, and the long dismantling of the system. Seventy-seven nooses hang from the ceiling for the hanged.
_facts: Opened November 2001, part of the Gold Reef City complex
Visitors are assigned race classification on entry and sent through separate gates
Located on site of former Crown Mines
77 nooses displayed representing people executed by the apartheid state

s: Anti-apartheid theatre in a fruit market
w: Barney Simon opened it in a disused Indian fruit market in 1976 — the year of the Soweto uprising — and ran plays the censors hated: Fugard, Gordimer adaptations, raw township drama. The government considered it subversive; audiences considered it a lifeline. The main stage was renamed for John Kani in 2014.
_facts: Opened 1976 in former fruit and vegetable market (Old Indian Market or Newtown Market)
Founded by Barney Simon and Mannie Manim as independent anti-racist theatre
Athol Fugard's plays among those staged here
Main Theatre renamed John Kani Theatre in 2014

w: The red-brick compound on Rahima Moosa Street once housed 4,000 migrant mineworkers, sleeping sixteen to a room, locked in at night. The Electricity Supply Commission built it in 1913; the City bought it in the 1980s and converted it into a museum of its own grim history. The dormitory blocks survive intact.
_facts: Built 1913 by the Electricity Supply Commission as migrant worker compound
Housed up to 4,000 mineworkers in cramped dormitories
Workers were locked in at night under the compound system
Located on Rahima Moosa Street, Newtown

s: Lutyens gallery marooned in a troubled park
w: Lady Florence Phillips — wife of Randlord mining magnate Lionel Phillips — commissioned Edwin Lutyens to design it, and it opened in 1915 as South Africa's first public art gallery. The collection runs to over 9,000 works. Joubert Park went rough for decades; the gallery kept its doors open.
_facts: Commissioned by Lady Florence Phillips, wife of mining magnate Lionel Phillips
Designed by Edwin Lutyens, opened 1915
First public art gallery in South Africa
Collection of over 9,000 artworks — once the largest on the continent

s: Where a young lawyer became Mahatma
w: Mahatma Gandhi worked as a lawyer from buildings on this block between 1893 and 1914, arriving as Mohandas and leaving — twenty-one years later — as the Mahatma. The square was called Commissioner Square until 2002, when the city renamed it and installed a statue. The honorific was first applied to him here in 1914.
_facts: Gandhi worked as a lawyer in Johannesburg from 1893 to 1914
The square was formerly known as Commissioner Square
Renamed Gandhi Square in 2002 with a statue installed
The honorific 'Mahatma' was first applied to Gandhi in South Africa in 1914

s: The brick house that changed everything
w: Nelson Mandela moved into 8115 Vilakazi Street in 1946 — a small brick house in Orlando West that became ANC command centre, safe house and family home. He was arrested in 1962 and did not live here again: 27 years of Robben Island followed. Two doors up the same street, Desmond Tutu's house still stands.
_facts: Mandela lived at 8115 Vilakazi Street, Orlando West from 1946 to 1962
Arrested in 1962 and spent 27 years imprisoned, primarily on Robben Island
Desmond Tutu's house (Tutu House) is a short distance up Vilakazi Street
Now operates as the Nelson Mandela National Museum

s: Africa's largest transport museum, surprisingly good
w: Founded in 1964 by Jimmie Hall and the City of Johannesburg, it has grown into Africa's largest transport collection: ox wagons, horse trams, steam locomotives, early motor cars and Johannesburg's first fire engine. Set beside the Wemmer Pan lake in Pioneers' Park, La Rochelle.
_facts: Established 1964 by Jimmie Hall and the City of Johannesburg
Largest transport museum in Africa
Located at Pioneers' Park beside Wemmer Pan lake in La Rochelle
Collection spans hundreds of years of South African transport history

s: Descend into an actual 1886 gold mine
w: Shaft 14 of the Crown Mines went down 220 metres into the reef that made Johannesburg. The mine closed in 1971 after ninety years of extraction; the shaft was preserved and is now the centrepiece of a theme park evoking the 1886 gold rush. You can descend into it and watch molten gold poured into a bar.
_facts: Crown Mines closed in 1971 after nearly 90 years of operation
Theme park opened on the site themed around the 1886 Witwatersrand gold rush
Visitors can descend Shaft 14 into the mine
Gold-pouring demonstrations take place on site

w: Johannesburg's only full-time independent cinema opened inside the 44 Stanley arts complex in Milpark — a repurposed block of garages and light-industrial workshops. Single screen, 102 seats, committed to local and world cinema the multiplex circuit ignores. Film talks, festivals, weeknight double bills.
_facts: Located at 44 Stanley Avenue, Milpark (Cottesloe)
Only dedicated independent cinema in Johannesburg
Single screen with approximately 102 seats
44 Stanley is a repurposed light-industrial complex
_sources: Nominatim geocode: The Bioscope Independent Cinema, 44 Stanley Avenue, Cottesloe, Johannesburg

w: Freehold suburb where Black Africans could own property — a loophole that lasted long enough to spawn a culture: Can Themba, Bloke Modisane, Miriam Makeba, the Drum writers. In 1955 the government bulldozed it and renamed the rubble Triomf, triumph. The original street grid survives under the bungalows.
_facts: One of the few areas where Black Africans could own freehold property pre-apartheid
Home to writers Can Themba, Bloke Modisane, Es'kia Mphahlele, Todd Matshikiza, and Miriam Makeba
Demolished by government in 1955 under the Group Areas Act
Renamed 'Triomf' (triumph) after demolition; original street grid still visible

s: Joburg's past in a converted fruit market
w: The old Newtown Market fruit sheds — they sold oranges and cabbages here from 1913 — now hold South African social history: the Treason Trial, the 1913 and 1946 mineworkers' strikes, photographs of Sophiatown before the bulldozers. The building is cavernous; the exhibits are intimate.
_facts: Housed in the former Newtown Market fruit sheds (operating from 1913)
Contains exhibitions on the Treason Trial and mineworkers' strikes
Photographs document Sophiatown prior to 1955 demolition
Historical museum in Newtown, Johannesburg

s: Africa's tallest tower for fifty years
w: Built between 1968 and 1971 at a cost of 2 million rand, the 269-metre tower was Africa's tallest structure for eleven years and the continent's tallest telecommunications tower for fifty — a record it still holds. Originally named for apartheid PM JG Strijdom; rechristened the Telkom Joburg Tower in 2005.
_facts: Construction 1968-1971, cost 2 million rand
Height 269m; tallest structure in Southern Hemisphere 1971-1978
Africa's tallest telecommunications tower — a record it still holds
Originally named JG Strijdom Tower after apartheid PM; renamed Telkom Joburg Tower 2005

s: The people's cathedral, bullet holes and all
w: Largest Catholic church in South Africa, built 1962 in Rockville. During the 1976 uprising, students fled police gunfire inside; the police followed and fired. Bullet holes in the floor tiles and altar were left as they were. Steve Biko's funeral was held here in 1977. Still called the people's cathedral.
_facts: Largest Catholic church in South Africa, designed by Anthony Noel Errol Slaven
Located in Rockville, Soweto
Police fired inside the church during the 1976 Soweto uprising
Steve Biko's funeral held here in September 1977

s: Bomb-blasted Edwardian pile on Rissik Street
w: Edwardian sandstone, built 1914 by Hawkey and McKinley; the Gauteng Provincial Legislature uses it now. The steps have seen protest meetings, rallies, and a bomb blast in 1988 — set off inside the building — that blew out the windows. The carved stonework above the entrance survived everything and looks it.
_facts: Built 1913-1914 by Hawkey and McKinley construction company
Edwardian design, plan drawn 1910
Currently houses the Gauteng Provincial Legislature
A bomb blast in 1988 damaged the building
